sections.title: Global Equities
sections.content: Global equities rose as a US-Iran peace deal sent oil sharply lower and reignited risk appetite, with mega-cap technology and semiconductors leading. MSCI World approx. +1.5% (approx., verify before merging), MSCI Developed Markets approx. +1.5% (approx., verify before merging) and MSCI Emerging Markets approx. +0.5% (approx., verify before merging), the latter restrained by subdued Chinese equities. The S&P 500 added 1.4% and the Nasdaq Composite 3.1%, with the MAG7 mega-cap cohort outperforming as technology led sectors at +4.4%; the Russell 2000 rose 3.9%. Europe outperformed, the Euro Stoxx 50 around +2.1% and the STOXX Europe 600 +3.0% in local terms. In Asia the Nikkei 225 edged up 0.39%, while Hong Kong's Hang Seng approx. +1% (approx., verify before merging) and China's CSI 300 was little changed (approx., verify before merging).

sections.title: US Equities
sections.content: US indices closed a volatile week higher as a US-Iran peace deal, announced over the prior weekend, drove crude roughly 13% lower and lifted risk sentiment, even as leadership narrowed back towards technology. The S&P 500 rose 1.4% and the Nasdaq Composite 3.1%, with gains concentrated in semiconductors and AI beneficiaries; technology led sectors at +4.4% and industrials +3.2%, while energy slumped 5.9% on falling oil. The equal-weight S&P 500 was essentially flat, underscoring thin breadth, though small-caps advanced, the Russell 2000 up 3.9%. The centrepiece was new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's debut FOMC, which held rates at 3.50-3.75% but delivered a hawkish message: the 2026 rate cut was removed from the dot plot, with around half of officials now seeing a hike this year.

sections.title: European Equities
sections.content: European equities outperformed, propelled by hopes of Middle East de-escalation and sharply lower oil following the US-Iran peace deal. The pan-European STOXX Europe 600 climbed 3.0% in local-currency terms, with Germany's DAX surging 3.92%, the UK's FTSE 100 up 2.66%, France's CAC 40 rising 2.05% and Italy's FTSE MIB adding 0.80%, lifting the Euro Stoxx 50 around 2.1%. Cheaper energy was read as a tailwind for the region's net importers and a salve for the recent inflation impulse, even after the ECB's prior-week rate increase. UK data softened the domestic backdrop, with the unemployment rate rising unexpectedly to 5% in the three months to March and job openings falling to a five-year low, while sterling-sensitive exporters benefited from a weaker pound after the Bank of England's policy decision.

sections.title: APAC Equities
sections.content: APAC markets were mixed, lagging the stronger gains in the US and Europe. Japanese equities were little changed, the Nikkei 225 edging up 0.39% while the broader TOPIX slipped 0.20%, as comments from BoJ Governor Ueda were read as raising the likelihood of a June rate hike, tempering enthusiasm despite the firmer global risk tone. Chinese equities were subdued: the CSI 300 ended little changed even after upbeat trade data, with May exports up 19.4% y/y and imports surging 27.4%, widening the trade surplus to USD 105.4bn, as investors awaited fresh domestic stimulus. Hong Kong's Hang Seng edged higher in line with the regional risk-on mood. Across Asia ex-Japan, lower energy costs offered relief to net importers, while the global technology cycle remained the dominant swing factor for sentiment.

sections.title: Foreign Exchange
sections.content: The US dollar surged to one-year highs, driven by the US-Iran peace deal and a hawkish debut from Fed Chair Warsh, whose decision to hold rates while signalling that further hikes are more likely widened the rate-differential case for the greenback. EUR/USD fell around 0.4%, unable to draw lasting support from the prior week's ECB hike, while GBP/USD dropped roughly 0.6% following the Bank of England's policy decision. USD/JPY rose about 0.5%, extending gains above 160.00 despite BoJ rate-hike speculation, as the dollar's broad momentum dominated. Warsh's notably shorter statement, light on forward guidance, reinforced a higher-for-longer narrative that underpinned the dollar across the board, leaving the peace-deal risk tone and the repricing of Fed expectations as the week's defining FX drivers.

sections.title: Commodities
sections.content: Commodities were dominated by a sharp unwind of the geopolitical risk premium after the US-Iran peace deal, with crude falling roughly 13% on the week as shipping began returning to the Strait of Hormuz and energy-supply fears eased. Prices found a late floor when planned US-Iran nuclear talks were postponed, Washington's envoy delaying a trip after Tehran cancelled the meeting, but the dominant theme remained sharply lower oil and cooling inflation pressure. Gold stayed on the defensive, trading around USD 4,464, roughly USD 230 below its 200-day moving average, as market focus shifted from energy-supply concerns to a more hawkish Fed; key support sits near USD 4,000-4,100. The cross-asset read was clear: cheaper energy supported risk appetite but redirected attention squarely to monetary policy and real yields.

sections.title: Fixed Income
sections.content: Government bonds sold off as the Fed's hawkish hold reset rate expectations. New Chair Warsh's FOMC kept the funds target at 3.50-3.75% but removed the previously projected 2026 cut from the dot plot, lifted inflation forecasts, improved its labour-market assessment and trimmed near-term growth, with about half of officials now pencilling in at least one hike this year. Markets read the message as more hawkish than expected, pushing yields higher and led by the front end: the 2-year UST rose to around 4.21% while the 10-year moved near 4.49%, flattening the curve. The takeaway, in Edward Jones's view, is that rates may stay higher for longer without the Fed yet signalling a renewed tightening cycle. Credit held a firm tone, with IG and HY spreads broadly steady amid the risk-on backdrop.
